question: Which of the following best describes static routing?
correct: Routes are manually configured by an administrator and do not change unless edited
incorrect: Routers exchange routing updates with each other to learn paths dynamically
Routes are automatically generated based on observed traffic patterns
Routes are discovered by broadcasting ARP requests for each destination
feedback: Static routes are hand-configured and do not adapt to topology changes. They are simple and predictable but do not scale to large or frequently changing networks.

question: Which of the following are advantages of dynamic routing compared to static routing?
correct: Routers can adapt automatically to topology changes such as link failures
Route tables scale more easily in larger networks
Admins do not need to update every router by hand when a subnet is added
incorrect: Dynamic routing protocols use no router CPU or bandwidth for updates
Dynamic routing removes the need for any IP addressing plan or summarization
Dynamic routing requires every router to use the same serial number
feedback: Dynamic routing trades some CPU and bandwidth overhead for automatic adaptation and scalability. Static routing remains useful for small or predictable topologies.

question: Captain Doran is peering Silverkeep Holdings' edge router with the ISP to exchange routes between their autonomous systems. Which routing protocol is he using?
correct: Border Gateway Protocol (BGP)
incorrect: Open Shortest Path First (OSPF)
Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP)
Routing Information Protocol version 2 (RIPv2)
feedback: BGP is the path-vector protocol used between autonomous systems on the internet. OSPF and EIGRP are interior gateway protocols used inside a single organization.

question: Which routing protocol is a link-state interior gateway protocol that floods link-state advertisements (LSAs) within an area and calculates shortest paths using Dijkstra's algorithm?
correct: OSPF
incorrect: BGP
EIGRP
RIP
feedback: OSPF is a link-state IGP. EIGRP is a Cisco advanced distance-vector (hybrid) protocol. BGP is a path-vector protocol used between autonomous systems.

question: A router has learned the same destination network from OSPF and from a static route. Assuming default values, which route will be installed in the routing table?
correct: The static route, because it has a lower administrative distance
incorrect: The OSPF route, because link-state protocols always win
Both routes, load-balanced equally
Neither route, because the tie cannot be broken
feedback: Administrative distance (AD) ranks the trustworthiness of route sources. Lower is better. Default ADs: directly connected = 0, static = 1, EIGRP = 90, OSPF = 110, RIP = 120.

question: A router has two routes to the same destination in its routing table: 10.1.2.0/24 via OSPF and 10.1.0.0/16 via BGP. A packet arrives destined for 10.1.2.50. Which route is used?
correct: 10.1.2.0/24, because it is the longest (most specific) prefix match
incorrect: 10.1.0.0/16, because BGP has a higher administrative distance
Neither, because the router will drop the packet
Both routes, in a round-robin fashion
feedback: The longest prefix match wins, regardless of routing protocol or administrative distance. A /24 is more specific than a /16.

question: Match each routing protocol to its default administrative distance on a Cisco router.
feedback: Administrative distance determines which source is preferred when the same destination is learned from multiple protocols. Lower AD wins.

question: Within a single routing protocol, when multiple valid paths to the same destination exist, what is used to pick the best one?
correct: The metric, which varies by protocol (cost for OSPF, composite metric for EIGRP, hops for RIP)
incorrect: The MAC address of the next-hop interface
The DNS name of the destination
The alphabetical order of the route's description
feedback: Administrative distance chooses between different protocols; metric chooses between paths within the same protocol. Each protocol defines its own metric.

question: Which of the following are true about NAT and PAT?
correct: NAT translates private IPs to a public IP at the network edge
PAT maps many internal hosts to one public IP by using different source ports
Home internet routers commonly use outbound PAT to share one public IP
incorrect: NAT operates at Layer 2 by translating MAC addresses rather than IP addresses
PAT requires each internal host to have its own unique public IPv4 address
NAT and PAT are only used on IPv6 networks and not IPv4
feedback: NAT rewrites IP addresses as packets cross a boundary. PAT (port address translation, a.k.a. NAT overload) multiplexes many internal hosts onto a single public IP using unique source ports.

question: The NAT variant that lets many internal hosts share a single public IP address by tracking unique source port numbers is called ___.
correct: PAT
port address translation
NAT overload
feedback: PAT (also called NAT overload) uses the transport-layer source port to distinguish flows sharing the same translated public IP.

question: Which technology lets two or more routers share a virtual IP that hosts use as their default gateway, so that if the active router fails, a standby router takes over transparently?
correct: First Hop Redundancy Protocol (FHRP) — e.g., HSRP, VRRP, GLBP
incorrect: Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)
Port Address Translation (PAT)
Link Aggregation Control Protocol (LACP)
feedback: FHRPs like HSRP, VRRP, and GLBP present a virtual IP/MAC that hosts use as their gateway. If the active router fails, a standby takes over without clients reconfiguring.

question: A load balancer presents a single IP address to clients, behind which a pool of real servers actually handles the traffic. What is this client-facing address called?
correct: Virtual IP (VIP)
incorrect: APIPA address
Loopback address
Anycast multicast group
feedback: A Virtual IP is a logical address owned by the load balancer (or FHRP group) rather than a single physical interface. Clients connect to the VIP and the traffic is distributed to real servers.

question: Aria has a single physical router interface connected to a trunk and wants it to route between three VLANs. Which feature does she configure to give the interface multiple logical IP addresses, one per VLAN?
correct: Subinterfaces with 802.1Q encapsulation (router on a stick)
incorrect: Spanning Tree Protocol
Port Address Translation
Jumbo frames
feedback: Subinterfaces (GigabitEthernet0/0.10, 0/0.20, etc.) let one physical interface terminate multiple VLANs by tagging each with 802.1Q — the classic 'router on a stick'.

question: Which of the following are true about VLANs?
correct: VLANs segment a single physical switch into multiple broadcast domains
Hosts in different VLANs cannot communicate without a Layer 3 device
VLAN membership information is stored in the switch's VLAN database
incorrect: All VLANs on a switch share a single broadcast domain by default
VLANs eliminate the need for IP addressing
Every VLAN on a switch must use the same IP subnet
feedback: A VLAN is a Layer 2 broadcast domain. Communication between VLANs requires routing (a Layer 3 switch SVI or an external router).

question: A Layer 3 switch needs an IP address on VLAN 20 so that it can route traffic for hosts in that VLAN. Which construct provides that IP?
correct: A Switch Virtual Interface (SVI)
incorrect: A physical routed interface
A trunk port
A native VLAN
feedback: An SVI (e.g., 'interface Vlan20') is a logical Layer 3 interface on a switch that provides a gateway address for the VLAN's hosts.

question: Which standard defines the 802.1Q tag inserted into Ethernet frames to identify VLAN membership on a trunk link?
correct: IEEE 802.1Q
incorrect: IEEE 802.3
IEEE 802.1X
IEEE 802.11
feedback: 802.1Q defines VLAN tagging. 802.3 is Ethernet itself, 802.1X is port-based authentication, and 802.11 is wireless LAN.

question: On an 802.1Q trunk, which VLAN carries untagged traffic by default?
correct: The native VLAN
incorrect: The voice VLAN
VLAN 0
The highest-numbered configured VLAN
feedback: Frames on the native VLAN are sent untagged across the trunk. By default this is VLAN 1, but best practice is to change it and ensure both ends of a trunk agree.

question: Which switch feature tags IP phone traffic into a dedicated VLAN while still carrying untagged PC traffic on the same port, so a phone and a workstation can share a single access port?
correct: Voice VLAN
incorrect: Native VLAN
Subinterface
Spanning Tree PortFast
feedback: A voice VLAN allows an IP phone (with a passthrough port for a PC) to tag its own voice traffic while the PC's data stays untagged on the access VLAN.

question: Which of the following statements about link aggregation are correct?
correct: It bundles multiple physical links between devices into a single logical link
It increases available bandwidth and provides redundancy if a member link fails
LACP (IEEE 802.3ad) is a common protocol used to negotiate the bundle
incorrect: It allows a single frame to be split across multiple member links simultaneously
It requires member links to operate at different speeds
It is a substitute for Spanning Tree and eliminates the need for STP on any network
feedback: Link aggregation (LAG / EtherChannel / port-channel) bundles links using LACP or PAgP. Individual flows are hashed to a single link, but the bundle as a whole looks like one high-bandwidth link.

question: Brom reports that a workstation connected to a 1 Gbps switch port is performing poorly. Running `show interface` on the switch shows the port negotiated at 100 Mbps / half-duplex. What is the most likely issue?
correct: A duplex and/or speed mismatch between the switch port and the NIC
incorrect: The switch does not support Ethernet
The port is configured as a trunk
Spanning Tree is blocking the port
feedback: Half-duplex on a modern switch port almost always indicates a failed autonegotiation or a mismatch (e.g., one side hardcoded, the other auto). It causes late collisions and poor throughput.

question: What is the primary purpose of Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) on a switched Ethernet network?
correct: To prevent Layer 2 loops by blocking redundant paths while keeping a loop-free active topology
incorrect: To distribute IP addresses to hosts automatically
To translate private IPs to public IPs at the network edge
To authenticate users connecting to a switch port
feedback: STP (and its successors RSTP/MSTP) blocks redundant links until needed, preventing broadcast storms and MAC table instability on networks with physical loops.

question: The standard Ethernet maximum transmission unit (MTU) for the payload of a frame is ___ bytes.
feedback: Standard Ethernet MTU is 1500 bytes. Jumbo frames typically carry a 9000-byte payload and require that every device in the path support them.

question: Which term describes an Ethernet frame with a payload significantly larger than the standard 1500-byte MTU, typically 9000 bytes?
correct: Jumbo frame
incorrect: Runt frame
Giant frame (error)
Baby giant
feedback: Jumbo frames reduce per-packet overhead for bulk transfers like storage traffic. Every hop in the path must support jumbo frames or fragmentation/drops will occur.

question: Which of the following are symptoms of an MTU mismatch between two hosts?
correct: Small packets (such as pings with default size) succeed while larger transfers stall
Applications that rely on large TCP segments hang or time out
Path MTU Discovery failures when ICMP is blocked along the path
incorrect: All ICMP echo requests are silently translated to broadcasts
The host loses its MAC address
DHCP leases are refused on every interface
feedback: MTU mismatches are classic 'small things work, big things don't' problems. Path MTU Discovery relies on ICMP Fragmentation Needed; filtering that ICMP causes 'PMTU black holes'.

question: On an 802.1Q trunk, traffic that is not tagged with a VLAN ID is assumed to belong to the ___ VLAN.
correct: native
feedback: Untagged frames on a trunk belong to the native VLAN. Mismatched native VLANs on either end of a trunk are a common misconfiguration.

question: On Cisco devices, the default administrative distance of a static route is ___.
feedback: Static routes have an AD of 1, beaten only by directly connected routes (AD 0). This is why static routes override OSPF, EIGRP, and BGP by default.

question: Match each switching term to its best description.

pairs.definition: A switch port that belongs to a single VLAN and carries untagged traffic for that VLAN
A switch port that carries traffic for multiple VLANs, tagged with 802.1Q
A logical Layer 3 interface on a switch that provides a gateway for a VLAN
Multiple physical links bundled into a single logical link for bandwidth and redundancy
A protocol that blocks redundant paths to prevent Layer 2 loops

feedback: These are the core building blocks of Layer 2 switch configuration for Network+.

question: Match each routing term to its best description.

pairs.definition: A value used to rank the trustworthiness of different route sources
A value used to choose between multiple paths learned from the same protocol
The number of bits in the network portion of a route, used to prefer more specific routes
A mechanism that allows multiple routers to share a virtual gateway IP for failover

feedback: Route selection is a layered decision: longest prefix match first, then AD across protocols, then metric within a single protocol.

question: Lyra is hardening the network and wants to make sure only authorized VLANs are carried on each trunk between switches. Which configuration technique should she use?
correct: Set an allowed VLAN list on each trunk so it only carries the required VLANs
incorrect: Disable 802.1Q tagging on every trunk
Move every trunk into the native VLAN
Enable PortFast on every trunk
feedback: Pruning the allowed VLAN list on trunks limits the blast radius of VLAN hopping and misconfiguration. Trunks should only carry the VLANs they actually need.

question: Which of the following describe why a network engineer might enable jumbo frames?
correct: To improve throughput on storage traffic such as iSCSI or NFS inside a data center
To reduce CPU overhead by sending fewer, larger frames for bulk transfers
incorrect: To force all devices on the internet to accept oversized Ethernet frames
To replace the need for Spanning Tree Protocol
To allow a switch to route between VLANs without an SVI
feedback: Jumbo frames are typically used on controlled, high-bandwidth links (storage networks, data center fabrics). End-to-end support is required, which is why they are uncommon on general internet-facing paths.
